explicit_text: I grew up in Benham, Kentucky, a small coal camp town sponsored by International Harvester. During the 1940s to 1960s, it was a close-knit community of about 3,500 people where everyone knew each other, and there was little class distinction despite knowing who held what position. Benham was part of the Tri-City Area in Harlan County, and it shaped my ability to work with people from all walks of life. I was even born in the Benham Hospital during the intense labor conflicts known as Bloody Harlan.

This town was part of the Tri-City Area in Harlan County, and it shaped my ability to work with people from all walks of life. I was even born in the local hospital during the intense labor conflicts known as Bloody Harlan.

explicit_text: International Harvester was the company that owned the coal mine in Benham, Kentucky, and it was unique because it was a captive mine, meaning all coal went strictly to them. They sponsored the Benham Credit Union starting in the mid-1930s during the Great Depression to help employees with banking needs. My father worked for International Harvester managing the Credit Union for 41 years, and the company played a big role in the community's economic life.

explicit_text: I was actually born and raised just outside the town of Benham in a small area called Clutts, spelled C-L-U-T-T-S. It was very remote and surrounded by mountains, and I grew up in the middle of the Gilliam clan, a family known for their mountain man traditions. Clutts was part of my early life where I learned pioneer skills like churning butter and hog killings, and it gave me a deep connection to the mountain lifestyle.

explicit_text: Brunner Gilliam was a true mountain man from the area around Clutts where I grew up. He was known for walking the mountains with a gun and teaching us about the wilderness, including showing us rattlesnake holes. He had several children and grandchildren, including Little Joe, Joe McCauley, who he would take on hikes. Brunner's presence was a big part of my childhood adventures in the mountains.

explicit_text: Joe McCauley, whom we called Little Joe, was the grandson of Brunner Gilliam, the mountain man who took us kids hiking around the mountains near Clutts. Joe was part of those childhood experiences where we learned about the mountain life firsthand. He was like family to us and part of the close-knit mountain community I grew up in during the 1940s and 1950s.

explicit_text: Harlan County, Kentucky, is where I grew up, specifically in the Tri-City Area that includes Benham, Cumberland, and Lynch. This county was the center of intense labor union conflicts known as Bloody Harlan during the 1930s and 1940s. The coal mining culture and the community spirit of Harlan County deeply influenced my upbringing and my understanding of mountain life and labor struggles.
implicit_text: The county in Kentucky where I grew up included the Tri-City Area that encompasses the coal camp towns of Benham, Cumberland, and Lynch. The coal mining culture and the community spirit of this county deeply influenced my upbringing and my understanding of mountain life and labor struggles.

explicit_text: The Tri-City Area in Kentucky consists of Cumberland, Benham, and Lynch, and it was the community hub where I grew up. Each town had its own identity: Cumberland was the trade area, Benham was the International Harvester coal camp, and Lynch was the U.S. Steel coal camp. Despite being close, there were rivalries especially in sports, and people rarely dated outside their own town. This area shaped much of my childhood social life in the mid-20th century.

explicit_text: Cumberland, Kentucky, was the trade area in the Tri-City region where I grew up. It was a bit different from the coal camps of Benham and Lynch, serving more as a commercial center. My grandmother even ran a small dress shop called Joanna's down in Cumberland in the mid-1950s, which was quite unusual for women in the mountains at that time. Cumberland was part of the broader community that raised me and shaped my early experiences.

explicit_text: Lynch, Kentucky, was the U.S. Steel coal camp in the Tri-City Area where I grew up near Benham. It was founded after International Harvester sold land to U.S. Steel, and it had a slightly different culture and rivalries with Benham. Lynch did not have a credit union until the 1960s, unlike Benham, which had one sponsored by International Harvester since the 1930s. People from Lynch and Benham rarely dated outside their communities due to these rivalries.

explicit_text: I was literally born during the period known as Bloody Harlan, which were violent labor union wars in Harlan County, Kentucky. On the day I was born in the Benham Hospital, the town was armed and six men had been shot due to conflicts between the United Mine Workers and the Progressive Mine Workers. My father even helped deliver me amidst this turmoil, and the violence left a lasting impression on my family and community history.

explicit_text: The United Mine Workers of America were one of the labor unions involved in the intense and often violent union wars in Harlan County, Kentucky, during the time I was born. They clashed with the Progressive Mine Workers in what became known as Bloody Harlan. These conflicts deeply affected the community of Benham and shaped the labor history of the coal mining region where I grew up.

explicit_text: The Progressive Mine Workers were a rival labor union to the United Mine Workers of America during the violent union wars in Harlan County, Kentucky, known as Bloody Harlan. They were active in Benham, where I was born, and their conflicts with the UMWA led to shootings and armed standoffs in the town. This rivalry was a defining part of the coal mining labor struggles in the 1930s and 1940s.

explicit_text: My father, Robert B. Hern Sr., came from New York State and had two years of education at Cornell University before moving to the mountains around 1937. He became the manager of the Benham Credit Union, which was sponsored by International Harvester, and he served in that role for 41 years. He was present at my birth during the Bloody Harlan labor conflicts and has always joked that my personality comes from that dramatic day.

explicit_text: My mother, Pauline Parker Hern, was a mountain girl born on the border between Harlan County and Bell County, Kentucky. She was the eldest child of Charles Ross Parker Sr., who started working in the mines at age twelve to support the family. My mother was very active in the community, and although women rarely worked outside the home then, her mother ran a small dress shop called Joanna's in Cumberland, which was quite progressive for the time.

His hard work and sacrifice were often talked about in our family, and he was a central figure in our mountain heritage.

explicit_text: Joanna's was a small dress shop in Cumberland, Kentucky, run by my grandmother in the mid-1950s. It was quite unusual for a woman in the mountains at that time to operate a business outside the home, so it was a big step forward for our family. I remember playing with the clothes there, and it was a fun and important part of our community life, showing early signs of changing roles for women in the area.

explicit_text: The Benham Credit Union was established in the mid-1930s and sponsored by International Harvester to provide banking services for coal miners during the Great Depression. My father became its manager and served for 41 years, helping employees save and borrow money when the local bank had collapsed. The Credit Union was a vital institution in Benham's community and economic life, especially during tough times.
